topic: Memory - Drop Flags and Conditional Drop

turns.content: I'm reviewing a script that uses std::mem::ManuallyDrop to guard against conditional initialization, but I noticed the compiler is still inserting drop flags in MIR even though the value was never fully moved. The code creates an option-like struct where fields are only initialized if a flag is set, yet the optimizer seems to treat it as fully owned regardless of that state.

You've hit on a subtle interaction between partial moves and drop flags in MIR. When you write code like this: let x = Foo::new(); if cond { use(x); } else { }, the compiler tracks whether each field has been initialized via drop flags. If a struct contains a partially-moved value, those fields remain valid but their ownership state isn't fully resolved until the move completes. The issue you're seeing likely stems from using ManuallyDrop incorrectly or misunderstanding how the drop flag propagates through nested types. Specifically, when you manually suppress dropping with ManuallyDrop, you must ensure the entire struct is treated as unowned in MIR to avoid spurious drop elaboration. If your code initializes fields conditionally without wrapping them in a proper Option<T> or similar pattern that tracks initialization state explicitly, the optimizer may assume full ownership prematurely.

topic: Memory - Drop Flags and Conditional Drop

turns.content: I'm porting a C library to Rust and I noticed that when I conditionally initialize a struct inside an `if`, the compiler seems to insert extra drop logic even though I only use it once. Why does Rust track initialization state this way?

The reason is that the Rust compiler uses drop flags in its internal representation called MIR to distinguish between values that are guaranteed initialized and those that depend on runtime conditions. When you write an `if cond { let x = Foo::new(); use(x); }` block, the value of `cond` might be false at some call sites, meaning `x` never gets constructed in memory at that point. The compiler therefore inserts a drop flag for `x` so it can safely omit the initialization step while still ensuring that if `cond` is true later, the destructor will run exactly once when control flow reaches the end of the block. This guarantees soundness because Rust promises to call drop on every constructed value regardless of how the code path was selected by the compiler or runtime.

That explains the safety model but I'm seeing partial moves where fields are still valid even though the struct itself is considered partially moved, and I want to suppress that flag entirely without changing my logic. How do I handle this?

You can use `std::mem::ManuallyDrop` to wrap your conditionally constructed value so it never acquires a drop flag in the first place. By wrapping `x` as `let x = ManuallyDrop::new(Foo::new())`, you tell the compiler that ownership of this field is intentionally deferred and should not trigger any initialization tracking or drop elaboration in MIR. This allows you to move individual fields out of the struct without triggering a full move, which is why partial moves are allowed as long as the root value remains wrapped in `ManuallyDrop`. If you remove the wrapper, Rust will treat the entire struct as partially moved and prevent further operations on it until all fields have been explicitly dropped or re-assigned.
